@krishnan-gopal 

> I think we need a --log-level argument for deciding the HDL-world verbosity, which is seperated from that of the Python world verbosity. I can imagine this is needed regardless of whether we use UVM or not.

I wouldn't mind having a standard option to do verbosity control from the command line. Ideally that option would accept a fixed set of values but the problem is that VUnit supports the creation of custom levels in addition to the standard ones and our levels differs from the levels in UVM. Another issue is that VUnit has started to move away from the concept that log levels has an order. The reason is that our log levels contains error related levels (warning, error, failure) and non-error related levels (trace, debug, info). It's a bit awkward to think of orders when you're mixing different things. Errors are more severe than warnings but there is no severity order between info and debug. Debug contains more details than info but it's not clear if warnings is more detailed information than debug or vice versa. And what happens if you add a custom status level? We still support the concept of a verbosity level but the primary concept of verbosity control is that levels at shown or hidden on an individual basis. What is the UVM approach?

Coming back to the discussion regarding exit strategy. Do you see a fundamental drawback with handing over the exit to the top level? Doing that doesn't prevent you from parsing the transcript in a post-hook and failing if errors are found. Did you test what happens when dereferencing a null pointer? I'm sure a transcript parser could find that but wouldn't it be simulator specific what error message to look for?

I am a bit concerned about the fact that the new output format potentially requires updates to existing commands. It will increase the friction when trying to add new commands to the CLI

- Contributors have to know that they need to test their command with the table output
- This is very much a domain specific feature in that one wouldn't know about this based on previous experience from other projects.

Ideally, the philosophy should be that we start with as few domain specific requirements as possible (basically any python method can be exposed in the CLI as long as you use scalar argument types) and things will work.

You can then optionally attach additional metadata to the command to get a *better* experience.

In this case, you *have* to attach additional metadata in order to make things work.

Additionally, I'd almost be interested to see `az acr create` command return the admin username and password when you specify the `--admin-enabled` flag. That way, you don't need to create the instance, and then immediately turn around to grab the generated credentials.

BulletPhysics wiki page for [MotionState](http://www.bulletphysics.org/mediawiki-1.5.8/index.php/MotionStates#Interpolation) says

>  **Interpolation**
> Bullet knows how to interpolate body movement for you. As mentioned, implementation of interpolation is handled through MotionStates.
 > If you attempt to ask a body for its position, it will return the position at the end of the last physics tick. That's useful for many things, but for rendering you will want some interpolation. Bullet interpolates the transform of the body before passing the value to setWorldTransform.
 > If you want the non-interpolated position of a body [which will be the position as it was calculated at the end of the last physics tick], use btRigidBody::getWorldTransform() and query the body directly.
 > To get the interpolated values for rendering it is necessary to call StepSimulation(deltaTime) in your rendering loop. Only include a deltaTime parameter, no other arguments. 

Seems like while debug drawing the physics world, BulletPhysics uses non-interpolated position of a body while the graphical mesh uses interpolated position. This difference causes lag while debug drawing.

Here is how I'm envisioning doing the UI for this. Will try to create some mockups soon too. This is a bit unstructured as I'm trying to just organize my thoughts on how to do this:

This UI will be implemented as part of the formgrade extension, rather than as an entirely new extension. There will be three main screens:

1. **Manage Assignments** -- the main view for editing assignments and releasing them. This will have a few sub-utilities:
    * *Edit Assignment Files* (editing the actual notebooks themselves)
    * *Edit Assignment Metadata* (e.g. change the due date). If the assignment is not already in the database then this should just display some default values, and if they are changed, then assignment should be added to the database.
    * *Generate Student Version* (runs `nbgrader assign`)
    * *Preview Student Version* (opens student version of the files)
    * *Release Assignment* (runs `nbgrader release`). Should have an option to "unrelease" an assignment, too.
2. **Grade Assignments** -- provides functionality for collecting and grading assignments. When assignments are first collected, before the submission information is in the database, they will be displayed but the option to access the formgrading interfact will be disabled until the autograder has been run.
    * *Collect Submissions* (runs `nbgrader collect`)
    * *Run Autograder* (runs `nbgrader autograde`) -- I think this is something that will only be allowable on a per-assignment basis, as running the autograder can sometimes take hours. Ideally in the future we might be able to queue tasks in a robust way so that grading all assignments simultaneously will be possible, but not initially.
    * *Manual Grading* (the existing formgrader functionality)
3. **Manage Students** -- shows the same view as what exists currently, but also provides the option to edit student metadata.

Also, if you add a configuration parameter for protocol (not sure how you'd prefer to do that, could actually make it a string that defaults to "http", or could have an SSL flag), you'll also likely want to have a parameter to allow nbgrader to be configured to either not verify the certificate (for simple self-signed certificates), or to accept a custom trust store (so you could specify the certificate's pem as the trust store, and at least verify that it is the self-signed certificate you are expecting).

jc,

I received your traceback error in the past,  nbgrader is looking for a header file to place at the beginning of the assignment.

In the nbgrader_config.py file, you will probably see this line:

c.IncludeHeaderFooter.header = 'source/header.ipynb'

If you comment out this line (# in position 1), it should produce the assignment without a heading.

I wanted the heading, so I provided a full path to the file and it worked fine:
The file should be in your source directory under your course directory.
Here is an example for my windows install:
c.IncludeHeaderFooter.header = 'C:\\Jupyter\\Notebooks\\courses\\CSC343\\source\\header.ipynb'

Hope this helps,

rd

@takluyver Ok, just fyi after some discussions in the numpy issue: It kind of does seem to be related to IPython, at least the error doesn't happen in the regular python shell, only in IPython/Jupyter notebook. But it's probably still an anaconda/MLK issue. The simplest example to reproduce an error like this in regular Python is with the following code:
```python
import IPython                   # or PyQt5.QtWidgets
import numpy as np
a = np.random.randn(2)
print(a.dtype)                   # float64
print(np.linalg.norm(a))         # 7.523412005985283 or some other real number
b = np.array(a, dtype=np.float32)
print(np.linalg.norm(b))         # nan / 0.0 / 6074001000.0 / 1.4142135 (i.e. not the right answer)
```
And in IPython the error happens without importing IPython first (as it is imported at startup).
I really don't know what in the world importing another library would do to mess up numerical results like this, but yeah, I guess there is still not really anything that you can do about it...

Hey! After reading through the different proposals and discussions I felt I should put my 2¢ into the mix. Excuse me if I've misunderstood something, it was quite a read! :)

## Flags

First off, I really like the name flags as it still feels in line with how the decorators work (when x and y is true do z). Seeing that state and status might be confused it's definitely good to have a different name.

Also opening up the possibility to attach objects to flags is a **great** idea! It won't hurt the simplicity but also not be limited to a simple on/off toggle.

#### Relation flags

One of the coolest features that Juju provides is relations but I've always struggled handling them in charms. If that could be simplified in any way that would be awesome! Relations should be made easy yet powerful.

One thing that I think could be good is to distinguish relations from flags a bit. It's good that relations can be handled like flags, but when declaring them it would be less confusing if they had their own namespace.

## Dependencies

One thing that I'd like to put emphasis on is dependencies. To make sure that an action that is depending on some previous steps is not being executed prematurely I've found myself having to keep track of which states are set and not set a lot. Example:

    @when(config_changed)
    def reconfigure():
        clear_flag(configured)
        clear_flag(available)

    @when_not(configured)
    def configure():
        status_set("maintenance", "Configuring server")
        ...
        if error:
            status_set("blocked", "Config error!")
        else:
            set_flag(configured)

    @when(configured)
    @when_not(started)
        ...
        set_flag(started)

    @when(started)
    @when_not(available)
    def available():
        status_set("active", "We are live!")
        set_flag(available)

At first glance, this looks pretty reasonable. The charm should do one action at a time and, if everything works well, eventually end up in the available handler where it turns green. But what happens if the user, for example, configures the charm with values that the workload refuses? In this case you probably want to output an error message. This is where the dependency-problem occurs:

1. `reconfigure()` clears the `configured` and `available` flag
2. `configure()` tries setting the new configuration but gets rejected, an error status message is set
3. `available()` is executed, since the `started` flag is still set, and the active status message hides the error

The easy fix for this would be to add the `configured` flag to the `available()` handlers `@when` decorator. However this can get messy very easily, introduce another two or three flags and all of your `@when` decorators becomes crowded quick.

I've discussed this briefly with @johnsca and I showed him the [quickfix](https://github.com/elastisys/layer-charmscaler-base/commit/cdd398af0d46cc9047d7850a9cdc2121c2fbe9cc) in the CharmScaler charm. Here, each state requires all states before it to be set before the handler can run.

A more full-fledged solution would probably model some form of dependency graph where a flag can depend on zero or more flags.

![dependency graph](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ad/Dependencygraph.png)

I also think a solution like this should solve the issue, that some of you have brought up with "preflight" or "linking states together", by enforcing order of handler-executions based on the dependencies. Also, implementing dependencies into the flag-model should not violate compatibility between 1.0 and 2.0 since the default flag/state would have no dependencies.

Triggers seem to be a step in the right direction but I feel that it might add more complexity than necessary for newcomers (something that _really_ should be avoided). I'm also not sure that it actually covers dependencies likes this completely, correct me if I'm wrong.

Thoughts?

Anyway I managed to fix the problem by myself. As you mentioned, session can lead to faster analysis, so I still use it in my code, and every time I finish analysing an app I dump it from the session.

The session still keeps huge memory even after I delete elements from the session, I did some test and found it was caused by python's OrderedDict. 

In session class, member variable *analyzed_files*, *analyzed_files* and *analyzed_files* are OrderedDicts. Somehow when contents are deleted from these three members, python holds their part of memory. Therefore my solution is to save the contents I want to keep first, rebuild those three OrderedDicts completely then put the desired contents back.

I believe the client already has the functionality you want, however, it's not presented in a very user friendly way as it's primarily been used for testing. The client has the following flags:
```
  --tls-sni-01-port TLS_SNI_01_PORT
                        Port number to perform tls-sni-01 challenge. Boulder
                        in testing mode defaults to 5001. (default: 443)
  --http-01-port HTTP01_PORT
                        Port used in the SimpleHttp challenge. (default: 80)
```
These flags allow you to specify for which ports the client sets up the domain validation challenges. In general, `--tls-sni-01` should be the port you've routed incoming port 443 traffic to and `--http-01-port` should be the port you've routed incoming port 80 traffic to.

You would not need to use both flags, however, standalone by default performs challenges over 443. You can control this behavior by using the following flag:
```
  --standalone-supported-challenges STANDALONE_SUPPORTED_CHALLENGES
                        Supported challenges. Preferred in the order they are
                        listed. (default: tls-sni-01,http-01)
```

We already have a naming policy for Fedora images that includes all the specified attributes:

https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/User:Adamwill/Draft_fedora_image_naming_policy

it's not perfectly observed at present, but we have thought about it. I didn't include disc number in that policy as it isn't really a present concern for Fedora, so I didn't think about it, but I believe that in practice,  if we actually built any multiple disc images for Fedora any more, the disc number would be in the filename.

According to releng, there are various technical obstacles to actually achieving full compliance with the policy; some of the tools just can't very easily produce images with the filenames we'd like. So I think trying to enforce a fully consistent file naming policy at the productmd level might be going a bit far at present.

So far as Fedora is concerned, I believe it's already basically a rule that compose deliverables must be uniquely named, at least until Koji namespacing is implemented; I'm not sure if the same applies to RHEL, but if it does, we *could* simply add a similar check that no two images within a compose have the same filename (just extend the check this commit adds to make sure they don't have the same 'unique' attributes).

FYI, the size of the terminal associated with the C's stdout isn't related to the IDLE shell. For example, in Linux when I run IDLE from the GUI, the associated terminal size is 0x0. 

On Windows, os.get_terminal_size uses the console API GetConsoleScreenBufferInfo. This can't work given IDLE has no attached console. Also, for a GUI app the Windows C runtime leaves the standard FILE streams uninitialized to an invalid file descriptor (-1), so Python's sys.__stdout__ is None. That's why you get an AttributeError complaining that NoneType (i.e. type(None)) has no attribute 'fileno'.

Currently shutil.get_terminal_size returns the fallback size when os.get_terminal_size(sys.__stdout__.fileno()) raises NameError or OSError. I think AttributeError and ValueError should be added to this list

You're true. Based on #148, we have *traditional* objects (markers/layers, etc) that are directly stored in the template variable `map.template_vars`, and we have plugins that are stored as objects in the dict `map.plugins`, and that have *render* methods for generating the html/css/js at the end.
And finally, everything is rendered at the same time with

    self.HTML = html_templ.render(self.template_vars, plugins=self.plugins)

In other words, `template_vars` stores objects that are rendered when they are inserted, and `plugins` stores objects that are rendered "at the end". We may progressively move *traditional* objects from `template_vars` to `plugins` (or whatever it's name) to reach a solution to your problem.

I would imagine 2 rules to avoid a terrible mess :

* When a plugin is inserted with `add_plugin/add_to_map`, it can read AND ALTER the map object (other plugins/marker/...)
* When a plugin is rendered with `render_html` and so on, it can only read the map object.

This will let us not bother about the rendering order of plugins ; but this will force the user to think about the order of insertions. For example, a plugin that sets all markers color to blue will only affect marker that were defined before it's insertion. While a plugin that counts the markers would eventually be able to count also the markers inserted after it's definition.

Ideally, I would add

    discriminator = 'field_name'

to the `Meta` of the Schema.

But if I do that, I don't know where to get `'discriminator'` back. It is lost in [`SchemaOpts.__init__`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L183) in Marshmallow, which only picks a whitelist of attributes.

Also, it is a bit misleading that [discriminator appears](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L196) in [`_VALID_PROPERTIES`](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L171), which is used in [field2property](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L280) but not in [schema2parameters](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/dev/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L289). AFAIK, it is only useful for a schema, not for a field.

Right now, what I do is add `'x-discriminator': 'field_name'` when calling `definition` on my schema:

    api.definition('MySchema')(schema, **{'x-abstract': True, 'x-discriminator': 'kind'}

This works because I added a schema definition registration helper to my framework to pass `'x-'` attributes to the spec: https://github.com/Nobatek/flask-rest-api/blob/master/flask_rest_api/spec/plugin.py#L111

The whole inheritance mechanism is [not well defined in OpenAPI](https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/403) yet so I only rely on `x-` properties.

Looking further into this issue, I think the appropriate action is to add conditions to `ModelChain.dc_model` to protect against incompleteness or disagreement between module parameter sets and the selected DC model. However, I think there is value in wrapping `pvsystem.retrieve_sam` with a `get_module_parameters(module name)` function in the as-yet-unwritten `pvlib.io_tools`.

The problem we'd like to solve here, is to test `module_parameters` against `dc_model` to see if all required parameters are there. 

Currently, we expect users to write into their main code two statements, e.g.,

```
sandia_modules = pvlib.pvsystem.retrieve_sam('sapm')
module = sandia_modules['Seraphim_Solar_System_SRP_275_6PA'] 
```

then pass `module` as an argument when instantiating `PVSystem`, and select the matching DC model when instantiating `ModelChain`. If there's a mismatch between `module` and `dc_model` then an error will occur in the DC model function when the sought-for parameter is not found. 

The only logical place in the current workflow to test the `module` keys against the `dc_model`, is at `ModelChain.dc_model`.  Here, both `model` (the model name string) and `system.module_parameters` are available, and we can test for sufficient inputs.  Barring objections, this is what I will do.

We could test at `pvsystem.sapm` but that would invite a precedent to test input at every function, which I think is overkill.

I think, in the long run, that many of the user difficulties with modules would be avoided by having `pvlib.io_tools` with a function `get_module_parameters(module name)` that would return module parameters (as a dict) if the module is found in the databases.

@mikofski thanks for that illustration.  I'm not opposed to adopting parts of pvfree into pvlib, as a replacement for the current method of reading csv files of model parameters.  But we'd want to agree that the change is needed and is an improvement.  I don't see that pvlib is ready for this change.  The problem is providing a traceable and stable input files.  The CEC databases contain module and inverter measurements, not model parameters.  The SAM libraries are output of processing the public CEC databases using tools that are now in the SAM SDK, but using those tools to produce the library files is currently somewhat ad hoc. Since we can't produce the model parameters natively in pvlib by processing the CEC databases, we rely on a particular SAM release to provide traceable and stable inputs. Because of this reliance on SAM, I think we're better using the files directly, than we would be with an additional layer of code between the files and pvlib.

I hope this makes sense.

DESCRIPTION

gmx mindist computes the distance between one group and a number of other
groups. Both the minimum distance (between any pair of atoms from the    
respective groups) and the number of contacts within a given distance are

contact instead of as multiple contacts. With -or, minimum distances to each
residue in the first group are determined and plotted as a function of residue
number.

plotted. This is useful for checking if a protein has seen its periodic image
during a simulation. Only one shift in each direction is considered, giving a
total of 26 shifts. It also plots the maximum distance within the group and
the lengths of the three box vectors.

But we don't care that it's float64 in between, we want that part to stay on CPU no matter what. I fear it might copy the shape to the GPU if we cast it to float32, then divide it by the scaling factor on GPU and copy it back to CPU -- I specifically removed the cast in #394 for that reason. I guess you shouldn't put too much emphasis on eliminating all float64 warnings!
Another idea: Does the warning also disappear if you cast the shape to float64? This wouldn't have any unintended side effects (except that it needlessly does a floating point operation in case you've got an integer scale factor).

> in my full case I cannot use them because I reshape to shapes like `cheight * cwidth`

Can you use `-1` for that, and `[0]` where you have `-1` now?

> The `cache` parameter that you suggested (#659 (comment)) would work great here.

How? The problem is that you're trying to do things in the constructor that should be done in `get_output_for()`, because you don't know the input expression before that. You're trying to construct a Theano expression directly, while a Lasagne network is meant to be a function for constructing Theano expressions.

> Is there another way to address that case currently?

The way it's intended would be to use a custom `MergeLayer` that has the internal `InputLayer` as an additional input, so it can grab its symbolic shape in `get_output_for()`. Writing a custom `MergeLayer` for each reshape is a bit cumbersome, though. We could have an `ExpressionLayer` that allows multiple inputs, or a `ReshapeLayer` variant that can refer to symbolic shapes of other layers.

There are a couple of changes we'd need, though:
* it should live in `layers/dnn.py`, as it requires cuDNN, and probably it should have DNN in its name then in case we ever add a non-cuDNN equivalent
* casting to float32 and back may result in the division taking place on GPU (haven't tested, but I think Theano is quite greedily moving computation to GPU). It'd probably be more efficient not to cast and use integer division (`//` instead of `/`). This will give you `floor`, for `ceil` you need to add the denominator minus one to the numerator.

Ok, you're right that it's not perhaps the main reason. The discovery mechanism of nose and pytest is perhaps a little more advanced than unittest's built-in one and the interface is much nicer. The provision of JUnit/coverage is indeed a very important thing and indeed all the third-party runners provide such features.

My aim is to make writing and maintaining tests as simple and easy as possible but automating as much of the work as possible. It also means using the tools and frameworks that the rest of the Python world is using as it relieves some of the pressure on maintaining our own code (see `GangaTest`).

When our main requirements are: automatically find tests, output junit xml, output coverage xml and run doctests I found that pytest came out on top. It also allows us to run tests in isolation in the future.

- Why does upgrading to pgsql 9.4+ solve the problem? **Because after PostgreSQL9.4 server have got JSONBin support**
- Does anything else need to be upgraded? **No**

It is true that the `scales` as defined in PyWavelets does not exactly correspond to `W0` in the equation.  This does not mean that the implementation in PyWavelets is incorrect, just that a different convention is being used to define the wavelet scales. As mentioned, above, to get 15 Hz in PyWavelets you should just need to choose the scale that causes `pywt.scale2frequency('cmor', scale) / sampling_period` to return 15 Hz.  The value you want for that case appears to be a scale of 3.33 when the sampling period is 0.01 s.  In other words, scale=3.33 in PyWavelets should be equivalent to W0=0.064 in the Torrence and Compo equation when `'cmor'` (specifically, `'cmor1.0-0.5'`) is used as the wavelet.

Well that is a bit wrong indeed...

Take your `Job` resolver for example, `Job.objects.filter(**args)` approximately equates to `Job.objects.all().filter(**args)`. And that is why you are not getting only *children* `Job`s.

Instead you should be putting each *children* resolver inside its *parent* node, and doing something like:

```python
class PushNode(DjangoObjectType):
    class Meta:
        model = Push
        filter_fields = ('revision', )
        interfaces = (relay.Node, )

    jobs = DjangoFilterConnectionField(JobNode)

    def resolve_jobs(self, args, context, info):
        Job.objects.filter(push=self).filter(**args)
```

And of course, remove all the unnecessary `all_*` queries from your root `Query` class.

The above code might not work, but should be enough for demonstration purposes ;)

I'll just note if you're in the dev branch you can load from a schema file like so:

```
with open('./schema.graphqls') as source:
    document = parse(source.read())
schema = build_ast_schema(document)
```

Schema will then be a GraphQLSchema object.

In order for this to work though, your schema.graphqls file has to have a Query type and a defined schema object like so:

```
type Query {
  hello(name: String): String
}

schema {
  query: Query
}
```

The basic approach is as follows (all references are in the above file):

1. Create a class which inherits from ``cmd2.Cmd`` - see **SubcommandApp** for reference

2. Create a pytest test fixture which creates an instance of this class and incorporates a StdOut hack which can then be passed to pytest unit tests - see **subcommand_app** for reference

3. Implement 1 to N pytest unit test functions which test the features you want using the above test fixture - see the six **test_subcommand_*** functions for reference

One thing to note: With this change `T* -> None` is allowed for both builtin and user-defined types (defined with `py::class_`). However, the other direction `None -> T*` is possible for user-defined types and `char*`, but no other builtins. `None -> T*` will never segfault like the issue here. It will present a proper `TypeError` with a useful message. 

So there's a bit of asymmetry there, which I'm personally fine with, but I'd just like to note it in case this might be an issue. Supporting `None -> T*` for builtins would also have non-trivial binary size and runtime costs, so I'd be agains supporting it.

Some ideas:
1. Port to click, which we've been dallying with anyway and which is based on optparse.
2. Drop Python 3 support, which would quickly fix the problem.
3. Drop Python 2 support, which many major projects (like Django) are now doing.
4. Keep a conditional argparse dependency, but build separate py2 and py3 wheels.

Click porting is time-consuming and full of packaging unknowns, so it's a long-term plan.

Dropping Python 2 is prettiest but will probably leave users of ancient distros behind. Do they deserve it? Perhaps. Will it make a more secure internet? Well…maybe, if it encourages them to upgrade! ;-)

Dropping Python 3 is meh, because we'll be forced to deal with it eventually.

My favorite short-term fix is (4). Because we're dividing on major versions, we shouldn't have the wheel-caching problems of #2343. Also because we're dividing on major versions, we don't need to depend on environment markers, which ancient pips don't support. Long-term, (3) or (1) would be nice. After messing around with things, this object-tree approach seemed to be the nicest way to do things, but I'm open to other suggetsions.

The new nginx parser actually constructs an object tree.
Assumptions about parsed list structure should be mostly hidden away in `parser_obj.py`, although certain decisions do leak to objects which consume the parsing objects. For instance, since the parsed objects take care of adding whitespace to a directive (such that it matches existing context), it expects an unspaced list to be passed into its functions.

In essence, the tree looks like:

```
                 root_file
                     |
                 Statements
                list of ...
                 /      \
ServerBloc---Bloc       Sentence---Include
              /                       \
      [name, contents]                file (Statements)
         /        \
    Sentence    Statements
```

The basic building block for all of this is the `Sentence`, which is simply a collection of tokens and whitespace. It acts a lot like the `UnspacedList` object from the previous parsing design-- `Sentence.words` magically hides the associated whitespace and quotation marks.

For future block/statement-like parsing (cough Dovecot cough), the basic Statements/Block/Sentence structures will stay the same. In order to add configurability, you'll be able to introduce parsing hooks (i.e. if hook returns True => parse as some type) in order to parse and define configuration-specific objects (like ServerBlocs in nginx).

`Statements` is a collection of Blocs and Sentences (blocks and directives). With a little bit of type introspection, recursive tree-searching operations become pretty easy to build and query. The API for this has to be fleshed out a little bit more, but the gist is that you can query for either Blocks or Sentences that pass a given filter, either shallowly or recursively.

`Include` directives actually load in and parse the requested file. In order to avoid re-parsing files that may have been included twice, we use the global map in `Context`.

## parse and get_data

`parse` parses these objects from the raw parsed lists, from `nginxparser`. `get_data` does the reverse, and recursively dumps the class into the original parsed raw-lists. Functions that modify these objects should modify the underlying raw-list structures as well, so that they play nicely with existing code and dump correctly into files.

## Context

Each object also tracks a `Context`, which contains contextual information like
 - the object's parent, used when duplicate blocks to automatically add that block as a sibling
 - the file where the object is from
 - the current working directory, used to discover files at relative paths that are included
 - a global map of other parsed files, used to prevent object duplication when following `Include`s
 - tabbing information

## Nginx-specific parsing

Most of the nginx-specific logic should occur in `ServerBloc` (TODO: I haven't finished making this abstraction super clean, but it should be), an Nginx-specific types of directives that necessarily change the way that particular statement is parsed. 
 - `ServerBloc` parsing and changes need to track a parallel `VirtualHost` object. Eventually, we might be able to consolidate the `VirtualHost` and `ServerBloc` objects, rather than keeping the two constantly in parallel.

## How does this solve our other problems?
Besides removing explicit parsed-list indexing from most of the nginx codebase, this also:

Put new Nginx server blocks we create in a new file when appropriate: #5185
 - Change the `context.filename` of the appropriate block before dumping.

Unquote parameters in Nginx: #5543
Support multiline concatenated quotes in Nginx: #5774
 - These can be done automatically with the `Sentence` abstraction.

nginx _add_directive should figure out spacing automatically: #3417
add_server_directives should pick up whitespace from context rather than having the caller set it, and definitely for replace it should just replace the contents: #5157
 - Keeping track of tabbing guesses in objects solves both of these.

## Notes

Some of the abstractions are not perfectly fleshed out/mumbled in the current code, but this can be ironed out.

With some work, I've gotten the existing code to play nicely with `configurator` and `obj`, although it involved more changes to those files than I would have liked (mostly involved removing all explicit parsed-list indexing). **Before continuing to massage the rest of the nginx codebase to play nicely with the new parser, I'm looking for comments/iteration on the high-level design.** The nginx code feels a lot more intuitive without the explicit list-indexing; however, this does touch *a lot* of previous assumptions throughout the codebase and I'm currently trying to make these changes less intrusive (so we don't rehaul everything at once).

If you have any questions or maybe thoughts on smoothing over the transition to the new parser, things that seem not-so-nice, ideas on how to improve the existing high-level design, additional edge-cases that need to be worked into the design, please let me know.

- Replace the copy of pydatalab in the container with your local copy, by using : `docker run -it -p "127.0.0.1:8081:8080" -v "${HOME}:/content"    -e "PROJECT_ID=<your-project>" -v "<path-to-pydatalab-clone>/datalab/:/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/datalab/"  gcr.io/cloud-datalab/datalab:local`

I don't think running unlocked eth node is more insecure than opening the key by ourselves. It may need additional setup step (to block external connections, etc), but I think it may have some advantages:
- we may/should rely on standard interfaces for signing, e.g. `eth_sendTransaction`, `eth_sign` and `eth_signTypedData` (when stable)
- we may rely on the eth node to give wider key support
- related to above, it'd be nice to keep the possibility of using hardware keys. Currently, signing is interactive, but with e.g. Trezor T/Core supporting custom firmwares, it could emerge an option to unlock/authorize once, auto-sign in the future, and would be nice if we support standard signing interfaces, so geth/parity/Metamask could be used to interface with those hardwallets
- if the user wishes, it may allow full interactive signing. It may not be practical for a full node, but the light-clients sharing libs could make good use of it

Obs: `personal_unlockAccount` isn't available in Parity out-of-the-box, but can be activated by enabling `personal` in Parity's interface, or `unlock`ing directly on parity command-line. Anyway, it's a nice interface for recurrent signatures which we should make use of.

Instead of forcing resource creation to linearize out all the many promises it depends on, this change attempts to have us await them in parallel.  The goal here is to reduce unnecessary linearization, as well as potentially expose parallization bugs that might exist elsewhere in the stack.

For example, say we have multiple dependsOn (Dep-1..., Dep-n) resources for some other resource 'R'.  If we linearize out waiting on these, then we may mask a scenario where the Dep-2 had an implicit dependency on Dep-1, and we didn't have a problem because 'R' linearized waiting for Dep-1 before moving to Dep-2.

By allowing more parallelization here, we will hopefully expose more issues, as well as open more opportunities for the engine being given more resource to work on simultaneously.

Ok, I tried starting the Redis servers in separate terminal windows, and right before the segfault is caught in gdb in the plasma manager, the Redis server (not the primary shard), prints `Killed`.

it looks like the problem was the following. The non-primary Redis shard was using more and more memory (I checked this in `top`). In this case the head node had about 7GB memory (c4.xlarge). Eventually the Redis shard used too much memory and was killed.

This led to a sigpipe happening in the plasma manager (which we ignored because of the call to `signal(SIGPIPE, SIG_IGN);`, we need to do this because sigpipes happen whenever a client disconnects), which then led to a segfault in the manager.

Running the same workload with a larger head node (m4.16xlarge) finished getting the one million tasks in about 1.5 minutes (though it's possible that the larger node size changed the number of tasks that were executed locally in a meaningful way).

**Note:** I'm running the same workload with 10 million tasks instead of 1 million. It finished after 23 minutes, but it looks like at some point the non-primary Redis shard dropped a subscription client (presumably the plasma manager on the head node). The problem could be that the plasma manager really isn't able to keep up with Redis since it is requesting notifications about several hundred thousand objects every second and it's also doing a bunch of computation to receive objects and also to receive notifications from the local object store.

**Note:** When running the manager in gdb, I noticed that it was very slow to process notifications about newly arrived objects from the local object store (e.g., a workload would fetch 100000 objects, and the objects would arrive pretty quickly, but then the manager would continue requesting object notifications from Redis as the notifications from the store slowly trickled in to the manager. Eventually it would receive all of the notifications from the store and would stop sending requests to Redis.

Twitch has added a new API version (v5) two months ago in mid november and announced that v1 and v2 will be removed **2017-02-14**. As I've said in my post above, not specifying an API version defaults to the latest version (which is v3), but this will change when v1/v2 have been removed. v5 will then become the default. There's also a deadline for v3, **2018-02-14**.

> ACTION REQUIRED: Twitch API Version Update
> TL;DR: Read all of this. It’s important and will break your integrations. We’re changing how the Twitch API works and old versions of our API will be deprecated and removed.
> 
> Today, we’re delivering a change that you’ve requested for quite some time! Twitch API v5 is available now and uses IDs to reference users instead of usernames. This new API version puts us in a better position to add new functionality and features to both the API and the Twitch website.
> 
> You can start using v5 and upgrading your integrations today. You simply pass the application/vnd.twitchtv.v5+json header on your requests. We’re working hard to deliver reference documentation on the affected endpoints by mid-December. Here is a quick example:
> 
> Retrieving a user in v3
> curl -H 'Client-ID: XXXXX' \
> -H 'Accept: application/vnd.twitchtv.v3+json' \
> https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/users/dallas
> 
> Retrieving a user in v5
> curl -H 'Client-ID: XXXXX' \
> -H 'Accept: application/vnd.twitchtv.v5+json' \
> https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/users/44322889
> 
> There are other changes to note with this version:
> The _links object is removed from all responses. As a developer, you will be responsible for handling the paging offsets when polling the API.
> Added sub-second precision to time fields.
> Removed /games/featured endpoint.
> Removed delay, background, and banner from the channels response object.
> Replaced q and type parameters on the search endpoint. The q parameter is now called query. The type parameter is always set to suggest.
> Removed paywalled field from the video response object.
> Removed /kraken/chat, /kraken/chat/emoticons, and /kraken/chat/emoticon_index.
> 
> What does this mean for me as a developer?
> You need to migrate your code to use v5. On February 14, 2017, we will be removing v1 and v2 of the API and transition v3 into a deprecated state. In one year (February 14, 2018), v3 will be removed from the API. We hope that this gives enough transition time for your integrations.
> If your integration relies on username now, you can use the /users endpoint to translate from a username to an ID as shown below.
> 
> curl -H 'Client-ID: XXXXX' \
> -H 'Accept: application/vnd.twitchtv.v3+json' \
> https://api.twitch.tv/kraken/users/dallas
> For questions regarding this transition, please ask on the developer forum thread. We know this is a big transition and want to help in any way that we can.
> 
> Thank you for being a part of the Twitch Developer community!

> I'd like to download the stream to a file, and watch it at the same time

See https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/issues/105#issuecomment-256678800 and the related questions which are linked there at the bottom of the thread.

There's also #920 which implements a record option (write to file and launch player at the same time), but this pull request still has some unresolved issues and hasn't been merged yet.

> So far, I've tried -o name.mp4 with mp4, mvk, and flv formats, but when I catch up to live, VLC closes.

These are just file name extensions and have no impact on the actual stream data, namely the container and its video/audio streams. If you're watching Twitch streams, you'll get a MPEG-TS streams (HLS protocol) containing h264 video and aac audio data.

> Which formats or flags should I use to let myself catch up without VLC closing

If you want to pause and resume a live stream later on, you should set a larger caching value in the player. This of course depends on the player and the transport method used by Streamlink.

In VLC (see its [command line parameters](https://wiki.videolan.org/VLC_command-line_help/)), you can define custom `--file-caching` or `--network-caching` values. I don't use VLC, so this might be wrong, but I think this is what you're looking for:

```bash
$ streamlink --player vlc --player-args '--file-caching 300000 {filename}' URL QUALITY
or
$ streamlink --player vlc --player-args '--network-caching 300000 {filename}' --player-passthrough http URL QUALITY
```

> Can I just --ringbuffer-size 2048M

This should only work if the player doesn't read any data from Streamlink while being paused. I wouldn't use this method, as you won't be able to jump around in the timeline of the resumed live stream, since your player didn't actually cache the data, but instead Streamlink, which will just continue to feed the data to the player from where it stopped. Don't quote me on that, though.

The `--ringbuffer-size` parameter desperately needs a better documentation...

If you are asking how to create a socks5 proxy, that would be with [direct_tcpip_ex](http://ssh2-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/session.html#ssh2.session.Session.direct_tcpip_ex). I believe this issue is mostly specific to (legacy) command prompts which use code pages (CP). The typical culprit is Windows Command Prompt. Also, Python 3.6 and above doesn't seem to have this problem as it replaces characters not in the active code page, just as you suggest in the Expected Behaviour.

Moreover, this issue is a real pain to solve. There's no way we can anticipate characters not in the CP being printed to the console, moreover there isn't really a pythonic/efficient way of replacing said characters before printing them.

There are a bunch of workarounds available to you though:
 1. If you're using windows command prompt, do `chcp 65001`
 2. Use PowerShell or any other command prompt which doesn't have this issue
 3. Upgrade to python 3.6

I'm leaving this open for now but I don't think we're going to see a proper fix for this implemented, I'm sorry.

Thinking about this more:
- The fundamental problem for tracking of cancel scopes and other contexts within an asyncgen is knowing whether it's currently suspended by a yield (to its async caller) or by a trap (to the event loop) yielded by some other async function it awaited. An asyncgen can't directly yield to the event loop, because "yield" in the body of an async function syntactically means yield to asyncgen caller. So any event loop trap must be behind an await().
- We know when asyncgens are created and destroyed, if we install the hooks appropriately. We can scope the lifetime of an asyncgen to the lifetime of the task that created it, as explained above. So, whenever a task traps to the event loop, we know what asyncgens might be running at the time of the trap.
- We can iterate over each of these asyncgens and check its ag_await member. If this is None, the asyncgen is suspended by a yield to its caller and we shouldn't consider its contexts to be in effect at the time of the trap. If it's not None, the asyncgen is suspended by awaiting someone else and we should consider its contexts to be in effect at the time of the trap.
- The missing piece is figuring out which contexts (pertaining to some task-local state like the cancel scope stack) "belong" to the asyncgen and which "belong" to the code that's calling it. I'll think about this some more.

I just pushed a commit that skips the test for now on PyPy. I'm not super happy about this, because the test is valid and important. But this is an important feature that I don't want to get blocked by PyPy, and this has a few advantages over `allow-failures`:
* With allow-failures, no-one will notice if other stuff breaks on pypy
* In fact, probably no-one will ever look at the pypy results at all, because when a PR is green then who clicks on the detailed results?
* And the pypy bug was causing freezes and then timing out after 10 minutes, which substantially increases travis's total turnaround time.
* I version-gated the skip logic, so even if we forget about it then eventually a new version of pypy will come along and start running the test again

Ideally, if we could create a simple integration test to cover the new functionality. I think as a starting point we could create a fairly straight-forward test with the following steps:
1) create an Elasticsearch (ES) domain and index
2) create a firehose stream configured with the ES domain/index as destination
3) manually put some records onto the firehose delivery stream (using boto3's firehose `put_record` method)
4) verify that the entries eventually end up being written to Elasticsearch
5) optional: connect the Firehose stream to a data source like an S3 bucket, and test the end-to-end integration (putting an object to S3, verify that it lands in Elasticsearch via the delivery stream).
